sanskrit: श्री भगवानुवाचअशोच्यानन्वशोचस्त्वं प्रज्ञावादांश्च भाषसे । गतासूनगतासूंश्च नानुशोचन्ति पण्डिताः ॥
hindiTranslation: श्रीभगवान् बोले  तुमने शोक न करनेयोग्यका शोक किया है और पण्डिताईकी बातें कह रहे हो परन्तु जिनके प्राण चले गये हैं, उनके लिये और जिनके प्राण नहीं गये हैं, उनके लिये पण्डितलोग शोक नहीं करते ॥
englishTranslation: The Blessed Lord said: You grieve for what should not be grieved for, yet speak words of wisdom. The wise do not grieve for the dead or the living.
essence: Clear seeing ends grief where the wise know none is needed.
speaker: Krishna
simpleMeaning: Arjuna is mourning people who should not be mourned, while speaking as if he understands wisdom. Krishna says the wise do not grieve for either the dead or the living.
simpleInsight: It brings relief by showing that clear seeing can hold loss without collapsing into sorrow.
detailedMeaning: On the battlefield between two armies, Arjuna has frozen. His bow has fallen, and Krishna begins by correcting the confusion itself: Arjuna is speaking like a wise man while reacting like someone lost in grief. This verse starts the teaching that follows.
modernRelevance: You stand beside a hospital bed, rehearsing the perfect words while your chest tightens. The mind reaches for philosophy, but the heart is still caught in fear and loss.
translationLiteral: श्री भगवानुवाच (the Blessed Lord said) / अशोच्यान् (for the ungrievable) / अन्वशोचः (you have grieved) / त्वम् (you) / प्रज्ञावादान् (words of wisdom) / च (and) / भाषसे (you speak) / गतासून् (the departed breath) / अगतासून् (the not-departed breath) / च (and) / न (not) / अनुशोचन्ति (grieve) / पण्डिताः (the wise)
insight: It shifts the mind from emotional confusion to clear discrimination about what truly deserves grief.

quoteVariants.text: You grieve where grief does not belong. The wise do not mourn the living or the dead.
Grief misses the mark. The wise do not mourn.
I’m grieving what wisdom would not grieve.
See clearly. Grief has its place.
